class_schedule.topic: Leading Technology Change in Educational Organizations
learning_objectives: Apply change management frameworks to educational technology adoption
Identify common barriers to technology adoption in schools and organizations
Develop strategies for building stakeholder buy-in
readings: Rogers, E. - Diffusion of Innovations Ch. 1 (overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: The most common reason educational technology initiatives fail is not technical — it is organizational. This course prepares you to lead technology change by understanding the human, political, and cultural dimensions of adoption.
key_concepts: Kotter's 8-step change model applied to ed tech adoption
Rogers' diffusion curve: innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, laggards
Barriers: resistance, inadequate training, poor infrastructure, misaligned incentives
Building coalitions: identifying champions and empowering change agents
The implementation dip: performance temporarily declines during adoption
lecture_notes.demonstration: Change scenario analysis: given a failing ed tech initiative, diagnose which Kotter steps were skipped and develop a recovery plan
setup_requirements: Case study of a failed technology initiative

review.value: When new technology is introduced, performance often dips before improving. Leaders who do not anticipate this may abandon promising initiatives prematurely.
Target early adopters first. They have credibility with colleagues and will become ambassadors. Trying to convince laggards first wastes resources and creates visible resistance.

learning_objectives: Develop a multi-year educational technology strategic plan
Align technology investments with institutional mission and goals
Create technology roadmaps with phased implementation
readings: ISTE - Essential Conditions for Technology Integration

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategic planning ensures technology investments serve educational goals rather than chasing trends. A good technology plan answers: Where are we? Where do we want to be? How do we get there? How will we know when we arrive?
key_concepts: Environmental scan: SWOT analysis for ed tech
Vision and mission alignment: technology as means, not end
Technology roadmap: phased implementation over 3-5 years
Budget planning: capital, operating, training, and replacement cycles
Stakeholder engagement: faculty, students, IT, administration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Strategic plan workshop: develop a 3-year technology plan outline for a hypothetical school district including vision, goals, phases, and success metrics
setup_requirements: Environmental scan data for a sample district

review.value: Every technology investment should trace back to a strategic goal. If you cannot explain how a technology purchase improves teaching, learning, or efficiency, it should not be purchased.
Hardware depreciates. Budget for 4-5 year replacement cycles from day one. Organizations that buy technology without planning for replacement face crisis spending later.

learning_objectives: Apply project management methodologies to ed tech implementations
Build and lead cross-functional technology teams
Manage vendor relationships and technology procurement
readings: PMI - Project Management for Educational Technology (adapted)
Practical guide to technology vendor evaluation

lecture_notes.introduction: Ed tech leaders manage complex projects involving diverse stakeholders, tight budgets, and technical dependencies. This session covers the project management skills needed to deliver technology initiatives on time, on budget, and to specification.
key_concepts: Project management phases: initiation, planning, execution, monitoring, closing
Agile vs. waterfall for ed tech projects
Cross-functional teams: instructional design, IT, faculty, accessibility
Vendor management: RFP process, SLA negotiation, contract management
Risk management: identifying and mitigating implementation risks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Project plan exercise: create a project plan for an LMS migration including timeline, milestones, resource allocation, risk register, and communication plan

review.value: Ed tech projects require instructional designers (pedagogy), IT staff (infrastructure), faculty (content), accessibility specialists (compliance), and administrators (policy). Excluding any group leads to failure.

Request for Proposal documents must specify requirements clearly, include evaluation criteria, require demos and references, and mandate accessibility compliance. Vague RFPs lead to vendor-driven decisions.

class_schedule.topic: Budget and Resource Management for Ed Tech
learning_objectives: Develop and justify educational technology budgets
Calculate total cost of ownership for technology investments
Present business cases to leadership and funding bodies
readings: CoSN - Essential Framework: Smart Education Finance

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology leaders who cannot speak the language of finance will not get funded. This session teaches you to build, justify, and defend technology budgets in ways that resonate with administrators and boards.
key_concepts: Budget categories: hardware, software/licensing, infrastructure, personnel, training, support
Total cost of ownership: acquisition + implementation + operation + replacement
Funding sources: operating budgets, grants, bonds, e-rate, partnerships
Business case construction: problem, solution, cost, benefits, ROI, risk
Cost-benefit analysis for technology adoption decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Business case development: build a one-page business case for a proposed technology investment including problem statement, cost analysis, projected benefits, and risk assessment

review.value: A $30,000 LMS license may have a $150,000 total cost of ownership when you include hosting, integration, training, support, and migration costs. Decision-makers must see the full picture.
The federal E-Rate program provides $2-4 billion annually for internet access and telecommunications in schools. Understanding E-Rate eligibility is essential for K-12 technology leaders.

learning_objectives: Use data to inform technology adoption and discontinuation decisions
Design program evaluation frameworks for technology initiatives
Communicate data findings to non-technical stakeholders
readings: Mandinach, E. - A Perfect Time for Data Use: Using Data-Driven Decision Making

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective technology leaders make decisions based on evidence, not enthusiasm. This session covers how to collect, interpret, and communicate the data needed to make sound technology decisions.
key_concepts: Data sources: usage analytics, satisfaction surveys, learning outcomes, cost data
Key performance indicators for technology initiatives
A/B testing for educational technology decisions
When to kill a technology initiative: sunk cost fallacy
Data storytelling for leadership audiences
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data-driven decision exercise: given 6 months of usage data, satisfaction scores, and outcome data for a technology pilot, recommend whether to scale, modify, or discontinue the initiative

review.value: Money already spent is gone regardless of future decisions. A technology initiative that is not delivering results should be discontinued even if significant investment has been made. Throwing good money after bad wastes resources.
Useful KPIs: adoption rate, active usage (not just logins), student satisfaction, learning outcome improvement, help desk ticket reduction, cost per user, accessibility compliance rate.

class_schedule.topic: Ethics, Equity, and the Future of Ed Tech Leadership
learning_objectives: Apply ethical frameworks to technology leadership decisions
Ensure technology investments advance equity rather than widen gaps
Develop a personal leadership philosophy for educational technology
readings: Warschauer, M. - Technology and Social Inclusion Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology is not neutral. Every technology decision has equity implications: who benefits, who is excluded, whose data is collected, and who controls the narrative. Ethical technology leadership demands intentional equity focus.
key_concepts: Digital equity: access, skills, usage quality, and outcome equity
Technology decisions as equity decisions: procurement, deployment, support, discontinuation
Ethical leadership: transparency, accountability, student-centeredness
ISTE Standards for Education Leaders
Developing your leadership philosophy: what you stand for and how you lead
lecture_notes.demonstration: Leadership philosophy exercise: draft a personal educational technology leadership philosophy statement addressing your values, equity commitments, and decision-making principles

review.value: Access to devices and internet is necessary but not sufficient. Equity requires equal access to skilled instruction, quality digital content, authentic learning activities, and ongoing technical support.
Five areas: equity and citizenship advocate, visionary planner, empowering leader, systems designer, connected learner. These define what effective ed tech leadership looks like in practice.

quizzes.topic: Change Management, Strategic Planning, and Project Management

questions.question: A school district purchased 1,000 tablets two years ago, but only 30% of teachers use them regularly. Using Kotter's change model, identify which steps were likely skipped and recommend corrective actions.
questions.answer: Likely skipped steps: (1) Creating urgency — teachers were not shown why tablets would improve their teaching or student outcomes. (2) Building a guiding coalition — technology champions among faculty were not identified or empowered. (3) Generating short-term wins — early successes were not celebrated or shared, so the majority never saw evidence of value. (4) Anchoring in culture — tablet use was not integrated into expectations, evaluations, or daily workflows. Corrective actions: identify the 30% of active users as champions, document and share their success stories, provide hands-on training led by peers (not IT), create a small-wins campaign showcasing student outcomes, and ensure administrators visibly support and model tablet use.

rubric.key: Connects skipped steps to the observed adoption failure

questions.question: Why is it important to include replacement cycle costs in a technology budget? A school buys 500 Chromebooks at $300 each. What is the 8-year total cost if the replacement cycle is 4 years?
questions.answer: Replacement cycle costs are critical because hardware depreciates and eventually becomes unsupportable. Without planning for replacement, organizations face crisis-level spending or students use obsolete devices. Calculation: Year 0: 500 × $300 = $150,000 (initial purchase). Year 4: 500 × $300 = $150,000 (replacement). Total 8-year hardware cost: $300,000. This does not include annual costs for cases, charging carts, management software, insurance, and IT support, which could double the total. Organizations that budget only the initial $150,000 will face an unfunded $150,000 expense in year 4.

rubric.key: Notes additional ongoing costs beyond hardware

questions.question: A technology pilot shows high teacher satisfaction (4.2/5) but no improvement in student test scores after one semester. A superintendent wants to cancel the initiative. What additional data should you gather before making a decision, and why?
questions.answer: Additional data needed: (1) Usage depth — are teachers using the technology for transformative activities (SAMR modification/redefinition) or just substitution? Surface-level use would not be expected to improve outcomes. (2) Time horizon — one semester may be insufficient to see outcome changes; implementation science suggests 2-3 years for mature implementation. (3) Student engagement data — are students more engaged, producing better work, or developing 21st-century skills not captured by standardized tests? (4) Comparison fairness — were test scores measured with assessments aligned to the new instructional methods, or do they only measure content taught the old way? Making a decision based solely on one semester of test scores and satisfaction ratings risks the sunk cost fallacy in reverse: abandoning a promising initiative before it has time to mature.

rubric.key: Identifies at least two types of additional data needed
Explains why one semester may be insufficient
Considers whether assessments are aligned to new methods

questions.question: Explain how a technology procurement decision can be an equity decision. Give a specific example.
questions.answer: Every procurement decision distributes resources and access. Choosing a technology that requires high-bandwidth internet disadvantages students and schools in rural or low-income areas. Example: a district selects an AI tutoring platform that requires continuous internet connectivity and only runs on devices with 4GB+ RAM. Schools in affluent neighborhoods with strong WiFi and newer devices can use it fully. Schools in low-income neighborhoods with older devices and spotty connectivity cannot. The procurement decision has widened the equity gap. An equity-focused decision would require the platform to work offline, run on older hardware, and include Spanish-language support — or reject the product and find one that works for ALL students.

rubric.key: Explains how procurement distributes access unequally

major_assignments.title: Educational Technology Strategic Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive 3-year educational technology strategic plan for a real or simulated educational organization. The plan must include an environmental scan, vision and goals, phased implementation roadmap, budget projection, change management strategy, evaluation framework, and equity impact assessment.
objectives: Apply strategic planning frameworks to educational technology
Develop a phased implementation roadmap with realistic timelines
Create a budget projection with total cost of ownership analysis
Integrate change management and equity considerations throughout
deliverables: 3-year roadmap with phases, milestones, and success metrics

criteria: SMART goals with clear success metrics
Goals address teaching, learning, and operational efficiency
TCO-based budget with all cost categories
Identifies equity implications of all major decisions
Includes measures to prevent widening digital divides
Considers accessibility, language, and socioeconomic factors
Change management and equity integrated throughout
Professional quality suitable for board presentation

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on leadership, strategy, and management concepts

participation.description: Case analysis discussions, role-plays, and leadership exercises

midterm_examination.description: Covers change management, strategic planning, and project management

final_project.description: Strategic plan with board presentation simulation and Q&A defense

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected. Case discussions and leadership simulations require participation. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Strategic plan milestones must be met for peer review.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review and collaborative case analysis are integral. All strategic plans must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Data and sources must be properly cited. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. Spreadsheet software for budget projections. Presentation software for final project.
